title: The F1 Racer Problem: Why Nvidia Engineers Still Matter When Everyone Can Write Kernels

source.original: Jensen Huang – Will Nvidia's moat persist?

body_html:
One of the sharpest challenges to Nvidia's moat is that its biggest customers — hyperscalers like Google, Amazon, and Microsoft — have the engineering talent to write their own CUDA kernels, build custom software stacks, and potentially switch to alternative accelerators. If the customers who generate 60% of your revenue don't need your software ecosystem, what exactly is the moat? Huang has a vivid answer: Nvidia's GPUs are like F1 race cars, not Cadillacs.

The analogy is precise. A CPU, Huang explains, is a comfortable cruiser — anyone can drive it at a decent speed, and performance differences between drivers are small. But Nvidia's accelerators demand expertise to push to their limits. The architecture's complexity means there's a vast gap between naive utilization and peak performance. Huang claims that Nvidia's own engineers, working alongside AI labs, routinely extract 2-3x additional performance from partner workloads through kernel optimization. At fleet scale, that's the difference between profitable and unprofitable inference.

Huang also highlights Nvidia's growing use of AI itself to generate optimized kernels — a recursive advantage that compounds over time. The company's deep knowledge of its own architecture, combined with AI-assisted code generation, creates an optimization capability that even well-resourced hyperscalers struggle to replicate fully. It's not that customers can't write their own kernels; it's that Nvidia can write better ones, faster, because no one understands the hardware as deeply as its creator.

This argument extends beyond raw kernel performance. Huang points to the sheer volume of code underlying modern AI systems and the debugging challenge it creates. When something breaks in a training run, developers need to know whether the bug is in their code or in the platform. Nvidia's mature, battle-tested stack provides a trusted foundation that reduces debugging time — a practical advantage that matters enormously when GPU-hours cost thousands of dollars. The reliability of the stack is itself a form of value that custom alternatives must match.

The F1 racer framing reframes the competitive question entirely. It's not about whether hyperscalers can build alternatives to CUDA — it's about whether they can extract the same performance from the same hardware without Nvidia's co-design expertise. Huang is betting the answer is no, and that the performance gap is wide enough to justify Nvidia's margins even in a world where every major customer has its own kernel team.
